hero.title: lintai
hero.subtitle: Fast offline checks for repo-local AI instruction and execution surfaces. lintai helps teams verify skills, MCP configs, hook settings, client policy files, and adjacent trust boundaries before they are trusted in local workflows or CI.

features.title: Offline-first by default
features.description: lintai is built for local runs, CI gates, and private repositories where you do not want a cloud upload path just to review agent artifacts.

features.title: Deterministic findings with evidence
features.description: Stable rule ids, structured evidence, and predictable exit codes make lintai easier to trust in repeatable local and CI workflows.

features.title: Targets the files that actually steer agents
features.description: Skills, MCP configs, Claude and Cursor settings, hooks, and related repository-local policy files are the core scope in the current beta.

features.title: Built for CI and SARIF from the start
features.description: Text, JSON, and SARIF output let teams gate pull requests and keep lintai inside normal DevSecOps flows instead of inventing a parallel review loop.

features.title: Honest Stable vs Preview boundary
features.description: lintai does not pretend every signal is equally mature. Stable findings are the baseline trust bar, while Preview stays useful but explicitly non-baseline.

features.title: More than repository scans when you need it
features.description: scan-known, inventory-os, and policy-os help audit what local AI clients already have configured, without changing the core repo-scan story.

featuredRules.title: Unscoped Bash grant in AI-native frontmatter
featuredRules.description: Flags shared AI-native markdown frontmatter when tool grants expose broad Bash authority without a narrower reviewed scope.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: This is currently the strongest skills-markdown rule for ordinary community repos because it catches high-blast-radius shell authority without relying on vague prose heuristics.
featuredRules.evidence: 20 TP, 0 FP in the latest external validation wave.

featuredRules.title: Mutable MCP launcher in markdown setup docs
featuredRules.description: Flags setup-style markdown that teaches mutable package launchers for MCP tooling instead of a reproducible install or pinned path.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: It catches copy-paste setup guidance that can silently drift under users, which is one of the clearest operational risks in markdown-heavy agent repositories.
featuredRules.evidence: Top operational docs rule for MCP setup surfaces.

featuredRules.title: Mutable package launcher in committed Claude hook settings
featuredRules.description: Flags committed Claude settings when hook commands launch mutable package tooling instead of a reviewed, reproducible executable path.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: Committed hook settings act like executable policy. Mutable launchers there are easier to defend as a real operational smell than many broader markdown heuristics.
featuredRules.evidence: One of the strongest committed-config rules in the beta set.

featuredRules.title: Mutable package launcher in committed mcp.json
featuredRules.description: Flags committed MCP client config when it launches package tooling through mutable execution paths instead of a reviewed install surface.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: This is one of the cleanest config-level rules in the product because it matches an executable trust decision, not just documentation language.
featuredRules.evidence: High-confidence committed-config rule for MCP wiring.

featuredRules.title: Claude settings allow Bash(*) in a shared config
featuredRules.description: Flags shared Claude settings when permissions allow unrestricted shell access instead of a reviewed command allowlist.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: Bash(*) is one of the easiest shared-policy smells to explain in review because it turns a checked-in team policy into a broad shell grant.
featuredRules.evidence: Straightforward reviewable signal for shared Claude policy.

featuredRules.title: Claude settings allow Write(*) in a shared config
featuredRules.description: Flags shared Claude settings when permissions allow broad file mutation instead of a scoped write contract tied to known repo paths.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: Shared Write(*) is difficult to defend in code review because it grants mutation power far beyond the workflow a repo usually needs.
featuredRules.evidence: Strong team-policy smell with obvious remediation.

featuredRules.title: Claude command hook is missing an explicit timeout
featuredRules.description: Flags shared Claude command hooks that declare a command but omit a timeout, leaving the hook execution boundary unbounded.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: Timeout-free shared hooks are an operational smell because they can hang unpredictably across machines and make failures harder to review.
featuredRules.evidence: One-line remediation with immediate operational payoff.

featuredRules.title: Path-specific Copilot instruction uses an invalid applyTo glob
featuredRules.description: Flags path-specific GitHub Copilot instructions when applyTo looks present but contains a glob pattern that fails deterministic compilation.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: A broken applyTo can pass code review while silently failing to target the files the team thinks it covers.
featuredRules.evidence: High-value contract check for shared Copilot routing.

featuredRules.title: Cursor rule keeps globs while alwaysApply is true
featuredRules.description: Flags Cursor rule frontmatter when a rule claims global application but still carries path globs that no longer narrow its scope.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: This is a clean config hygiene rule because it catches contradictory routing in shared Cursor rule packs without any prose heuristics.

featuredRules.title: Cursor rule frontmatter contains an unknown key
featuredRules.description: Flags shared Cursor rule markdown when frontmatter introduces keys outside the supported Cursor contract.
featuredRules.whyItMatters: Unknown keys create the false impression that the editor honors extra routing or policy fields, which makes community rule packs harder to trust.
featuredRules.evidence: Tight frontmatter contract check with low ambiguity.

faq.question: What is lintai?
faq.answer: lintai is an offline-first, precision-first security linter for repository-local AI trust surfaces: skills, MCP configs, hook settings, agent rules, and adjacent client policy files.

faq.question: What does lintai scan in the current beta?
faq.answer: The current beta focuses on repository-local trust surfaces such as SKILL.md, CLAUDE.md, Cursor rules, MCP configs, Claude hook settings, local client config files, and the opt-in advisory lane for committed npm lockfiles.

faq.question: Is lintai really offline-first?
faq.answer: Yes. The core repo-scan workflow is designed for local runs and CI without live network lookups during scan. Even the advisory lane uses the bundled offline snapshot by default unless you explicitly point it at another normalized dataset.

faq.question: What is the difference between Stable and Preview findings?
faq.answer: Stable findings are the release-quality baseline. Preview findings are still useful, but they are not yet the default trust bar and should be evaluated as deeper guidance rather than the main gating signal.

faq.question: What is the fastest way to try lintai?
faq.answer: Install the public beta CLI from GitHub Releases, run lintai scan . on a repo that already contains supported AI-native files, and then compare the Stable vs Preview findings before deciding how hard to gate it in CI.

faq.question: Does lintai replace cloud scanners or broad supply-chain platforms?
faq.answer: No. The current beta is intentionally narrower. Think of it as a focused repo-local linter for agent trust surfaces, not a hosted platform for registry reputation, broad ecosystem crawling, or every possible AI security problem.

download.title: Get started
download.note: Pick an install path, open the docs, and start with a fast repo-local scan.

installChannels.title: Verified shell installer
installChannels.description: Recommended Unix-like install path for the public beta. The installer downloads the tagged archive plus SHA256SUMS and verifies the checksum.
installChannels.note: Best default on macOS and Linux when you want the supported beta flow.

installChannels.title: PowerShell installer
installChannels.description: Supported Windows beta path. Downloads the tagged archive plus SHA256SUMS and installs into a user-level bin directory.
installChannels.note: Use this on Windows when you want the same release-asset flow as the Unix installer.

installChannels.title: Direct archive + verify
installChannels.description: Manual path when you want the raw release assets and checksum verification without the convenience installer.
installChannels.note: Good when you want the release artifacts directly and will verify them yourself.

installChannels.title: GitHub Releases
installChannels.description: Browse published release notes, artifacts, and version history directly.
installChannels.note: Use this when you want the raw release surface.

installChannels.title: Docs index
installChannels.description: Open the rules, presets, and project docs published under GitHub Pages.
installChannels.note: Best next step once the CLI is installed.

quickstartSteps.title: Install the CLI
quickstartSteps.note: Use the install channel that matches your machine, then confirm the CLI is on PATH.

quickstartSteps.title: Run the first repo scan
quickstartSteps.note: Start on a repository that already contains supported AI-native files so the first run is representative.

quickstartSteps.title: Export SARIF for CI or code scanning
quickstartSteps.note: Use SARIF when you want the same scan integrated into CI and downstream tooling.

quickstartSteps.title: Inspect resolved policy when you add config
quickstartSteps.note: Useful once the target repo has a local lintai policy and you want to confirm the active preset and rule posture.

supportLanes.name: Repository-local scan surface
supportLanes.note: The current product story is the repo-local scan path for AI-native files such as skills, MCP configs, hook settings, and local client policy surfaces.

supportLanes.name: Stable vs Preview policy
supportLanes.note: Stable findings are the release-quality baseline. Preview remains useful but explicitly non-baseline and more context-sensitive.

supportLanes.name: Offline advisory lane
supportLanes.note: Dependency advisory matching is intentionally opt-in and limited to committed npm lockfiles against the active offline snapshot.

supportLanes.name: Installed artifact audit
supportLanes.note: scan-known, inventory-os, and policy-os extend lintai beyond repo scans when you need to inspect what local AI clients already have configured.
